relevance_reasoning: Primary dataset is microsatellite genotypes of Glyptemys insculpta (248 offspring, 2006–2007) — data type is genetic_analysis (high importance) but temporal coverage is short (2 years) and spatial extent is not reported, yielding moderate relevance; relevance upgraded because the species is IUCN-listed as endangered.

The authors provide primary capture/abundance data (raccoons and striped skunks) with georeferenced trap coordinates in Montérégie and Estrie, Québec, so data type is high-value (abundance). However, no temporal extent or overall spatial extent (km²) is reported in the text, so the dataset relevance is downgraded to low for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

Dataset is a primary telemetry-derived individual home-range and demographic dataset for woodland caribou in Québec spanning 1999–2011 (13 years), but the data type is not one of the high-priority EBV types (e.g., abundance or density) and the overall spatial extent is not reported. Therefore overall relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring is rated low.

Dataset comprises repeatability data on habitat selection for woodland caribou collected 2004–2011 (8 years, moderate temporal extent). The primary data are occurrence/used-location records for marked individuals (presence-only classification) so baseline data-type relevance is low, but relevance is upgraded because the study concerns a threatened species, yielding an overall moderate relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

The study provides primary microsatellite genotype data (genetic_analysis = high importance), covers multiple years (2011–2014, moderate temporal extent) but spatial extent in km² is not stated. Because spatial extent is unspecified and the special-case rule applies (genetic data with an unknown spatial extent), overall relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring is scored low.

Primary dataset is microsatellite genetic data (18 loci) for Acer saccharum populations in Québec, so data type is genetic. However, no temporal extent or spatial range (km2) is reported in the text, so overall relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring is rated low.

The primary dataset is microsatellite genetic data (genetic_analysis = high-value data type) collected 2010–2014 (5 years = moderate temporal extent), but the spatial extent is not quantified in the text. Presence of an at-risk species (Eastern Wolf) and documented range extensions justify upgrading to moderate relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

Primary data comprise abundance/proportion estimates from 15,339 ecological survey plots summarized across 535,355 km² (H for data type and spatial extent). No temporal extent is reported (X), and per the relevance rules the absence of temporal information with an otherwise H data type reduces the score to Low.

Primary data are GPS telemetry locations of 96 caribou (2007–2014) plus MODIS-derived ice/water time series (2000–2014); the dataset is a presence-only telemetry time series from northern Québec. Temporal extent is moderate (multiple years) but spatial extent in km² is not stated; northern-Québec focus upgrades relevance, giving an overall moderate relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

Dataset is microsatellite genotypes of Atlantic salmon sampled 2002–2005 (genetic_analysis = H). Temporal extent is 4 years (M) but spatial extent is not reported (X); applying the guideline for H with an unknown spatial extent yields a conservative overall relevance of L for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

Authors provide primary datasets (tree-level trait measurements and SNP genotype files) from a poplar hybrid stand in Quebec City, making the data directly relevant to Quebec biodiversity monitoring; however, the abstract and metadata give no information on temporal duration or spatial extent, which limits their broader monitoring applicability, so overall relevance is scored as low (L).

Authors collected abundance data for small mammals and beetles and measured vegetation structure at multiple sites in northeastern Québec, but sampling occurred in two years (2007 and 2011) with no spatial extent reported; short temporal coverage and unknown spatial extent reduce immediate relevance for province-wide long-term monitoring.

Authors collected individual-level body mass and breeding (hatchling/fledgling) count data for tree swallows over seven years (2005–2011) in Quebec, which provides useful temporal and trait/abundance information but the spatial extent and resolution are not reported in the text; therefore relevance for Quebec-wide biodiversity monitoring is rated low.

Long-term individual-based, georeferenced data on Tree swallows in southern Québec (≈10 years) provide temporal monitoring of breeding phenology at farm sites; temporal extent is moderate and spatial coverage is local, making it of moderate relevance for Québec biodiversity monitoring.

The authors collected quantitative plot-level species cover data (784 1-m2 plots) across 53 planted and 14 natural riparian sites, so data_type maps to abundance (H). However, the dataset appears to be a single sampling (no time-series) and the spatial extent (area in km²) is not reported, so temporal and spatial classifiers are X—yielding an overall Low relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

GPS-telemetry presence-only datasets for caribou, moose, and wolves spanning 2005–2009 provide multi-year (5 yr) temporal coverage but no explicit spatial area. Base relevance is lowered by presence-only data type but upgraded due to the study region (Côte-Nord, Québec) indicating a northern geographic focus important for Quebec monitoring.

The authors provide primary genetic datasets (microsatellites and mtDNA) for Myotis lucifugus (genetic_analysis), which scores highly by data type, but the abstract does not report the temporal sampling period or quantify spatial extent within Quebec; therefore overall relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring is assessed as low.

GPS telemetry time-series (2004–2010) on 49 female woodland caribou in Québec provides multiyear, individual-based movement data for a threatened species relevant to provincial monitoring; temporal extent (7 years) and species conservation status support high relevance.

The authors provide 24 seasonal habitat-quality raster maps (distribution outputs) for Québec caribou scenarios, which is directly relevant; however, temporal and spatial extents are not reported in the text, lowering relevance. Presence of an endangered (threatened) caribou population increases the relevance by one level.

The authors produced primary datasets of caribou capture/GPS locations (sample coordinates) and microsatellite genotype data (genetic_analysis), which is highly relevant in principle. However, temporal and spatial extents are not specified in the text, so overall applicability to Quebec-wide biodiversity monitoring is limited, yielding a conservative relevance score of L.

Primary dataset comprises plot-level abundance counts of regeneration (50 plots × 19 cutovers) sampled once five years after logging; temporal extent is a single post-harvest survey and spatial extent/area is not reported, so overall relevance for Quebec monitoring is low.

Primary dataset consists of physiological and swimming-trait measurements and PIT-tag passage data for three redhorse species collected at the Vianney-Legendre fishway (data type = traits). Temporal coverage is explicitly 2010 and 2012 (moderate temporal extent) but spatial extent is not stated, so overall relevance is scored as L (low) for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

The authors collected benthic community (abundance/diversity) data and habitat parameters at Sept-Îles, Quebec (site-level), so data type relevance is high; however the abstract gives no spatial extent or temporal duration, so overall relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring is rated low due to missing temporal and spatial extent information.

Authors report an experimental dataset manipulating HFS richness/abundance and measuring associated species establishment and community primary productivity over a 16-week period in the St. Lawrence estuary. Short temporal extent (16 weeks) and unspecified spatial coverage limit its applicability for broader Quebec biodiversity monitoring, so overall relevance is low.

Primary dataset is population genomic (GBS SNPs) for Arctic Char from Nunavik (23 sites, 650 individuals) but temporal and spatial extents in km²/years are not stated, yielding limited monitoring utility by the rubric; presence in northern Quebec (Nunavik) provides one upgrade but overall relevance remains low.

Authors collected primary data (7,107 individuals across 16 host species, parasite prevalence/intensity, mite OTU richness and COI barcodes) across 13 sites in southeastern Ontario and southwestern Quebec, giving multispecies, abundance and genetic data (high-value data types). However, no temporal extent or spatial area is reported, so relevance is moderate for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

The study reports primary abundance data (counts per leaf) which is highly relevant as a data type, but the temporal extent is short (2 years) and spatial extent is not specified; these limitations reduce relevance. Presence of multiple species (>10 listed) and two species recorded for the first time in Quebec increase its applicability to regional monitoring, yielding an overall moderate relevance.

The study reports primary abundance data (pitfall trap counts) and species-level identifications across multiple sites in Quebec, but no temporal extent (years) or area (km²) is provided. Given high-evidence data type but missing temporal and spatial extent information, overall relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring is assessed as low.

The authors produced spatially explicit future species distribution outputs and maps of species richness change for resident bird species in the boreal forest of Quebec, which are directly applicable to Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

Primary data are abundance counts of weevils collected over three consecutive years (1997–1999) at two vineyard sites (time-series, multispecies: 73 species). Spatial extent is not reported. Under the relevance rules this combination yields a moderate relevance (upgraded from low because of multispecies coverage and records new to Quebec).
